comments:
This paper reports an empirical comparison of ten standard classifiers on a sampled subset of the KDD99 intrusion detection dataset and proposes simple classifier-selection models that pick a classifier per attack category (and an alternate variant prioritizing low training time). Strengths: the paper evaluates a reasonably broad set of widely-used algorithms using a common toolkit (Weka) and it explicitly addresses practical deployment concerns such as runtime cost. The discussion is candid about KDD99’s limitations and about deployment trade-offs.

Major weaknesses that limit the value of the results: (1) Experimental design and reporting are weak. The training set is heavily imbalanced (e.g. only 13 U2R training instances) yet the paper draws conclusions about U2R and R2L performance — results for such rare classes are not statistically reliable. The sampling procedure is underspecified (how was sampling performed, were train/test splits stratified, were random seeds recorded?), and there is no evidence of cross-validation, confidence intervals, or statistical tests. (2) Metrics and reporting lack necessary detail: only TP/FP and an aggregate AA are presented; per-class precision/recall, confusion matrices, ROC/PR curves, and per-class support are not provided. (3) Some reported numbers and wording are unclear or inconsistent (e.g., phrases that appear to confuse TP/FP or give implausible rates). (4) The proposed selection models are simple rule maps (one classifier per attack type) and the improvements reported over baselines appear modest; no strong baseline ensembles or MCS methods are compared, nor is there analysis of when the selection model would generalize to other datasets. (5) Reproducibility is limited: exact Weka options are given for some classifiers but the overall experimental pipeline, preprocessing steps, and scripts or data splits are not provided.

Recommendations to improve the work: (a) redo experiments with properly stratified splits or cross-validation and provide per-class counts and confusion matrices; (b) address class imbalance (resampling, synthetic examples, or use of appropriate metrics like F1 and PR AUC); (c) include statistical significance testing for comparisons; (d) report runtime/inference throughput (not only training time) and memory usage to support deployment claims; (e) clean up presentation (remove repeated text, correct numeric/reporting errors) and publish the exact data splits and code for reproducibility; (f) compare against simple ensemble baselines (majority vote, stacking) or recent MCS approaches to establish whether per-class selection is better than standard ensembles.

Given the limited experimental rigor and the modest, incremental contribution, the paper may be of interest as a poster or short empirical note after substantial revision and clearer experiments.
